From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Person who wants to know or knows everything

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | It has been suggested that this article be **[merged](/wiki/Wikipedia:Merging "Wikipedia:Merging")** into *[Philomath](/wiki/Philomath "Philomath")*. (**[Discuss](/wiki/Talk:Philomath#Merge_proposal "Talk:Philomath")**) *Proposed since March 2026.* |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | The topic of this article **may not meet Wikipedia's [notability guideline for neologisms](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_neologisms "Wikipedia:Avoid neologisms")**. Please help to demonstrate the notability of the topic by citing [reliable secondary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources") that are [independent](/wiki/Wikipedia:Independent_sources "Wikipedia:Independent sources") of the topic and provide significant coverage of it beyond a mere trivial mention. If notability cannot be shown, the article is likely to be [merged](/wiki/Wikipedia:Merging "Wikipedia:Merging"), [redirected](/wiki/Wikipedia:Redirect "Wikipedia:Redirect"), or [deleted](/wiki/Wikipedia:Deletion_policy "Wikipedia:Deletion policy"). *Find sources:* ["Pantomath"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Pantomath%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Pantomath%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Pantomath%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Pantomath%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Pantomath%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Pantomath%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A **pantomath** is a person who wants to know or knows everything. The word itself is not to be found in common online English dictionaries, the *[OED](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, dictionaries of obscure words, or dictionaries of [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pantomath&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Pantomath "Special:EditPage/Pantomath") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(July 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A [pantomath](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pantomath "wiktionary:pantomath") (*pantomathēs*, παντομαθής, meaning "having learnt all", from the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") roots παντ- "all", "every" and the root μαθ-, meaning "learning", "understanding") is a person whose astonishingly wide interests and knowledge span the entire range of the arts and sciences.

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pantomath&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Uses")]

Pantomath is typically used to convey the sense that a great individual has achieved a pinnacle of learning, that an "automath" has taken [autodidacticism](/wiki/Autodidacticism "Autodidacticism") to an endpoint. As an example, the obscure and rare term seems to have been applied to those with an astonishingly wide knowledge and interests by these two authors from different eras: [Jonathan Miller](/wiki/Jonathan_Miller "Jonathan Miller") has been called a pantomath,[[2]](#cite_note-2) as has [Rupert Hart-Davis](/wiki/Rupert_Hart-Davis "Rupert Hart-Davis").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pantomath&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Omniscience](/wiki/Omniscience "Omniscience")
